segments.s388-g193: یکی is an indefinite subject meaning 'someone' or 'a certain one'. تیغ الماس گون is a noun with a postpositive modifier: تیغ 'sword' modified by الماس گون 'diamond‑like' (گون here = '-like'). برکشید is a past tense (3rd sing.) verb composed with the prefix بر‑ indicating an upward/drawing motion — here 'drew up' or 'drew (a sword)'.
segments.s388-g194: همی is a classical particle marking an ongoing or attempted action ('was about to / attempted to'). خواست is the past of خواستن 'wanted/intended' and here introduces the attempted action. از تن marks separation 'from the body'; سرش را is the definite object 'his head' with the possessive enclitic ش and object marker را. برید is the verb 'to cut off' (3rd sing. past) but its given lemma senses do not include 'cut', so its meaning here is 'cut (off)'.
segments.s388-g195: بترسید is the past (3rd sing.) of ترسیدن 'feared' and is placed before the proper name in the poetic inverted order (verb before subject). از گزند is a prepositional phrase meaning 'from/for harm' (i.e. 'feared harm').
segments.s388-g196: ز (poetic form of از) indicates source 'from'. فتراک is 'saddle‑cloth' (the place where the lasso was kept). بگشاد is an archaic past form meaning 'opened/loosened/unfastened' (here 'loosened out / produced'); پیچان is an adjective 'twisting/winding' modifying کمند 'lasso/snare', which is the object of بگشاد.
segments.s388-g197: به نام is a standard prepositional phrase 'in the name of'. جهان‌آفرین 'world‑creator' modifies or appositive to کردگار 'Maker/God', forming an invocation phrase used adverbially.
segments.s388-g198: بینداخت is a past tense verb 'threw/cast' (3rd sing.). بر گردن indicates the target location 'upon the neck'. گرگسار is an epithet meaning 'wolfish/ferocious' (used as a descriptor of the person whose neck receives the snare). The direct object (the snare) is omitted because it was introduced earlier.
segments.s388-g199: به بند اندر آمد is an idiomatic perfective phrase 'came into the bond' i.e. 'was fettered/put into bonds' (اندر آمد = 'entered into'). The following phrase سر و گردنش 'his head and neck' (with possessive ش) is the affected body part; word order places the verb phrase before the patient, common in poetry.
segments.s388-g200: بخاک 'to the ground' marks the destination. اندر افگند is a past action 'cast/put (into)' (اندر inserted for classical register). لرزان تنش 'his trembling body' is the direct object (possessive ش). Overall: '(they/he) cast his trembling body to the earth.'
segments.s388-g265: نیایش (noun) + همی کرد (compound verb) forms a past habitual/continuous action: “kept praying” or “prayed” (همی marks ongoing/habitual in older Persian, کرد = did/made). خود is emphatic/reflexive “himself” (emphasises the subject). با پدر is a prepositional phrase “with his father,” placed after the verb as in poetry to show accompaniment.
segments.s388-g266: بران is a demonstrative/adverbial (archaic) meaning roughly “to/unto that” or “upon that,” pointing forward; آفریننده دادگر is a noun + epithet: “the Creator, the just one.” The adjective/epithet دادگر follows the noun as an attributive in poetic word order.
segments.s388-g267: یکی هفته = “one week” (temporal phrase). بر پیش یزدان پاک is a locative/privative phrase: بر پیش = “in the presence of / before,” یزدان پاک = “the pure God” (یزدان = God, پاک = pure). The whole phrase gives the duration and the place/presence.
segments.s388-g268: همی بود is past continuous/habitual (همی + بود) — “was / remained.” گشتاسپ is the subject placed after the verb (poetic inversion). با درد و باک is a coordinated prepositional phrase “with sorrow and fear,” describing his state.
segments.s388-g269: به هشتم is a temporal phrase “on the eighth (day).” به جا آمد is an idiom meaning “came at the right/appointed time” (جا = place/opportunity, آمد = came). The proper name اسفندیار appears after the verb (poetic word order).
segments.s388-g270: بیامد = past ‘came/arrived.’ به درگاه او = “to his court / to his presence” (درگاه = royal court/presence, او = his/he). گرگسار is a trailing epithet/appositive meaning “wolf‑like / ferocious,” placed after the phrase as a descriptive tag.
segments.s388-g271: ز (poetic 'from/because of') introduces the cause: “because of / from.” شیرین روان is a compound epithet meaning “sweet‑souled Shirin” (شیرین used as a proper name, روان = soul/spirit) and functions as the cause. دل‌شده is a participial expression “heart‑made,” i.e. “smitten” or “wearing the heart” and ناامید is a predicate adjective “hopeless,” describing the mental state.
segments.s388-g272: تن از بیم لرزان = “(his) body trembling from fear” (تن = body; از بیم = from fear; لرزان = trembling). چو از باد بید is a simile “like a willow from the wind” (چو = like/as; باد = wind; بید = willow), completing the image of fragility.
segments.s388-g73: بفرمود is a past, honorific verb 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result 'that/so that'. هرچ is an old/dialectal form of هر چه 'whatever'. بد here is ambiguous and functions as an attributive element attached to خواسته (see below) rather than a standalone negative; the phrase هرچ بد خواسته together means 'whatever he desired/asked for' in this context. خواسته is a past-participial adjective 'desired/asked (for)'.
segments.s388-g74: ز ... و ز ... are poetic short forms of از meaning 'from'. گنج is the treasure/treasury and اسپان is the plural of اسپ (horses), here 'horses' or the cavalry mounts. آراسته is a past-participle/adjective 'adorned/provided', functioning predicatively: 'supplied from the treasure and from the horses' (i.e. taken from the treasure and the horses to furnish whatever was requested).
segments.s388-g75: ز چیزی introduces a partitive/indefinite 'of/anything that'. که is the relative pronoun 'that/which'. از + بلخ بامی is a genitival phrase 'from the roof of Balkh' (بامی = 'on the roof / rooftop'). ببرد is a past-subjunctive/relative verb form here meaning 'he had taken/carried away' (برد used with present/past narrative sense 'take/carry').
segments.s388-g76: بیاورد is the main verb 'he brought/produced'. یکسر is an adverb meaning 'entirely/altogether'. به introduces the recipient (to/for). کهرم here is the recipient (a proper/name or title); its dictionary senses given ('old age/ancient thing') do not fit straightforwardly as a common noun, so it functions as a name/title in context. سپرد is the past verb 'he entrusted/handed over'. Together: 'he brought it and handed it all over to [کهرم].'
segments.s388-g77: ز indicates source 'from'. کهرمش is کهرم + possessive suffix -ش 'his کهرم' (so 'from his کهرم'). کهتر is an adjective 'younger/smaller' modifying پسر 'son'; the sequence کهتر پسر is 'the younger son'. بد here appears as a proper epithet or name attached to پسر (not the ordinary adjective 'bad') and functions appositionally: 'the younger son, Bad.'
segments.s388-g78: بنه here means a small bundle/heap (not the pistachio sense) and بر + نهادند is the verbal construction 'they placed/laid upon' (نهادند = past tense 'placed'). و شد is a simple narrative 'and it became', and پیش بار is a compound meaning 'the load/burden placed in front' (پیش = before/in front of, بار = load/burden). So: 'they placed four small bundles on (it), and it became the forward load.'
segments.s388-g79: برفتند is a past narrative verb 'they departed/rode off'. بر here is a preposition/prefix 'on/upon/toward', هر سوی is a distributive phrase 'each/ every direction/side'. صد هیون literally 'a hundred beasts' (هیون = animals/beasts). The word order places the verb first (typical in epic narrative) and then specifies the distribution of mounts.
segments.s388-g80: نشسته is a participle/adjective 'seated/saddled' describing the beasts. برو here is a verbal/adverbial element meaning 'ready to go / going' (literally 'go'), functioning with نشسته to convey 'saddled and ready to go'. نیز is a conjunction 'also/likewise', and صد رهنمون means 'a hundred guides/leading-men' (رهنمون = guide). The whole segment lists the accompaniment: 'saddled and ready-to-go beasts, and likewise a hundred guides.'
segments.s388-g281: به لشکر (prepositional phrase) = 'to the army/host'; گه is a temporal noun meaning 'moment/occasion' and sits after the phrase to mark time. آمد is the past tense main verb 'came'. که here introduces the identification of who came (a classical use: 'that/who'), ارجاسپ is the proper name, and بود is the past copula. Together the clause literally orders as 'to the army (at the) moment came that Arjasp was' -> 'Arjasp came at that moment.'
segments.s388-g282: که begins a subordinate/relative clause ('that/where'); ریزندها here is a noun form (classical/poetic spelling) referring to 'those who scatter/are scatterers' or agents of scattering; خون is 'blood', لهراسپ is the name Lohrasp, and بود is the past copula 'was'. The clause describes a situation connected to the main clause (e.g. 'where blood of Lohrasp was scattered').
segments.s388-g283: ببخشید is the past tense (3rd singular) of بخشیدن and here means 'he pardoned/forgave' (classical narrative usage). زان = 'from that' (compound of ز + آن), رزمگه is 'battlefield', and خواسته is a participial/adjectival form meaning 'having asked/been requested' (i.e. 'those who had begged'). The phrase means he spared/released those who had pleaded at that battlefield.
segments.s388-g284: سوار و پیاده = 'horsemen and foot-soldiers' (sawar = riders/cavalry; پیاده here as noun = infantry). شد is the past of شدن meaning 'became' or 'were made', and آراسته is an adjective 'adorned/arrayed/prepared'. Together: 'the cavalry and infantry were arrayed/put in order.'
segments.s388-g285: سران و اسیران = 'the chiefs (leaders) and the prisoners'; که introduces a relative clause modifying them ('that/which'), آورده is the perfect participle/past form 'had brought', and بود is the past auxiliary 'was' used with آورده to form 'had brought'. So this segment names the people ('the leaders and captives that he had brought').
segments.s388-g286: بکشت is an archaic past-form verb here functioning as the main verb 'he killed' (classical compound/old form). آن = 'those/that', کزو = 'from whom/by whom' (classical fused relative/pronominal form), لشکر = 'the army', آزرده = 'distressed/afflicted', and بود is the past copula 'had been/was'. Together the clause gives the reason/identity: 'he killed those from whom the army had been distressed.'
segments.s388-g97: "بدو گفت" is a compound where "بدو" (to him/unto him) precedes the verb and marks the indirect object, and "گفت" is the past tense 'said'. "کای" is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone; "شیر پرخاشخر" is a noun phrase 'lion' + adjective 'quarrelsome/aggressive' (epithet) placed after the vocative.
segments.s388-g98: The clause uses the older/polite ordering "ترا هست X" (literally 'to you is X') meaning 'you have X'. Here "ترا" is the 2nd‑person object pronoun, "هست" is the copula/existential 'is/there is', and the three coordinated nouns "نام و نژاد و هنر" enumerate the qualities possessed (name/reputation, lineage, and skill).
segments.s388-g99: "گر" introduces a conditional 'if'. "این را که گفتی" is a topicalized object with a relative clause: "this (that) which you said" ("که" introduces the relative clause and "گفتی" is 'you said'). "بجای آوری" is a verbal phrase meaning 'bring to its place' → idiomatically 'fulfill/perform/bring to pass'; "آوری" is the present/subjunctive form used for potential/optative sense.
segments.s388-g100: Literal order: "هنر بر زبان رهنمای آوری" — "هنر" (skill) is the theme, "بر زبان" ('upon the tongue') is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in speech', "رهنمای" ('as a guide') modifies how the skill is presented, and the verb "آوری" repeats the idea of 'bring'/'make'. Together: 'make your skill serve as a guide in your speech.'
segments.s388-g101: "ز" (poetic form of از) marks origin/extent 'from', so "ز توران زمین تا به دریای چین" means 'from the land of Turan as far as the sea of China'; "تا" expresses extent 'as far as', and "به دریای چین" is a prepositional phrase 'to/unto the sea of China'.
segments.s388-g102: "ترا بخشم" is an idiomatic gift/promise construction: "ترا" (to you) + "بخشم" (I will bestow/give). The conjunction "و" links an additional item "بوم ایران‌زمین" ('the land/soil of Iran') as another thing being granted. Word order places the indirect object before the verb, common in classical style.
segments.s388-g103: "سپهبد تو باشی" uses the subjunctive/optative form "باشی" ('you be') to give an appointment or wish: 'you shall be (my) spahbed'. "به هر کشورم" ('in each of my countries') specifies the scope with the preposition "به" + distributive "هر" and the possessed noun "کشورم" ('my country/realm').
segments.s388-g104: "ز فرمان تو" ('from your command') marks the source/standard; "یک زمان" ('a single moment') is the temporal phrase. The verb "نگذرم" is a negated first‑person present-subjunctive meaning 'I will not pass' — here idiomatically 'I will not depart from/neglect your command even for a moment.'
segments.s388-g217: به marks the indirect object ('to the warriors'); گفت is the past tense verb 'he said/asked' introducing an indirect question; کهرم کجاست is the reported question ('where is Kahram') with کجاست = کجا + ست (copula) forming the embedded interrogative.
segments.s388-g218: درفشش is درفش + possessive -ش ('his banner'); نه precedes the predicate for negation ('is not'); پیداست is the adjective 'visible' with the copula; بر دست راست is a locative phrase ('on the right hand') introduced by the preposition بر.
segments.s388-g219: همان is a demonstrative/emphatic 'that/same' placed before the noun phrase; تیغ‌زن is a compound noun 'swordsman/attacker'; کندر here functions as a name or epithet placed in apposition; شیرگیر is an epithet 'lion-catcher' following the name.
segments.s388-g220: که is a relative pronoun 'whom/that' linking this clause to the preceding noun; بگذاشتی is 2nd-person past 'you placed/you had put' (the prefixed b- is narrative/old usage); نیزه is the direct object 'spear', بر کوه is a locative phrase 'on the mountain', and و تیر coordinates 'and (the) arrow' (ellipsis is possible, common in poetic listing).
segments.s388-g221: به ارجاسپ places the indirect object 'to Arjasp'; گفتند is the past plural 'they said/told' introducing reported content; کاسفندیار is the subject/name reported in that content clause.
segments.s388-g222: به رزم is an adverbial phrase 'in/into battle'; اندرون 'within/inside' specifies location; بود is the past copula 'was'; با introduces accompaniment 'with' and گرگسار is an epithet/noun 'wolf-like' or 'ferocious', describing those he was with.
segments.s388-g223: ز is the poetic/prepositional 'from/on account of'; تیغ دلیران is a genitive construction 'the swords of the brave' (tiġ + modifier); هوا شد بنفش is a simple clause 'the air became purple' with شد 'became' as the copula and بنفش as the resultant adjective — a metaphorical image (blood/tinted air).
segments.s388-g224: نه پیداست is the negated predicate 'is not visible' (نه before predicate); آن is the demonstrative 'that'; گرگ پیکر is a compound describing form/shape 'wolf-bodied' or 'wolf-formed'; درفش is the noun 'banner/standard' — 'that wolf-shaped banner is not visible.'
segments.s388-g169: چنین گفت is a formulaic report 'thus said' (چنین + گفت, گفت = past 'said'). کاین = که + این ('that this'), introducing the reported content. کین خون نیاست is a nominal clause with predicate نیاست ('is not'); word order is emphatic: کین (grudge/vengeance) + خون (blood) + نیاست (is not) = 'this grudge is not blood.'
segments.s388-g170: کزو is a relative/ablative 'from which / from it', linking back to the preceding clause. شاه را دل here expresses possession in a classical Persian order: 'شاه را دل' = 'the king's heart' (را marks the possessor as a definite/topic phrase). پر از + noun = 'full of ...', so پر از کیمیاست = 'is full of alchemy.'
segments.s388-g171: عنان را(bare noun + را) marks the direct object 'the reins.' بپیچید is past tense 'he twisted/turned' (ب‑ prefix here in an older narrative form). بر + میسره indicates direction 'toward/onto the میسره' (میسره is the noun that follows بر and marks the goal/direction).
segments.s388-g172: زمین شد is 'the earth became' (شد = past of شدن 'to become'). چو = 'like/as' introduces a simile; دریای خون = 'a sea of blood' (دریای is the ezāfe form of دریا). یکسره is an adverb 'completely/all at once' modifying the whole phrase.
segments.s388-g173: بکشت is an older narrative past form meaning 'he killed' (verb + past), followed by the partitive/ablative phrase از دلیران 'of/from the brave' which is the group affected. صد و شصت و پنج is the cardinal number 'one hundred sixty-five' (Persian numbers joined with و).
segments.s388-g174: همه نامداران = 'all the renowned (men)' (همه = all, نامداران = famed ones). با تاج و گنج = 'with crown and treasure' (با = with, a prepositional phrase qualifying those killed or describing what they had). The whole segment further characterizes the slain as illustrious and richly endowed.
segments.s388-g175: چنین گفت = 'thus said' (reporting formula). کاین کین آن سی و هشت = 'that this grudge that thirty-and-eight...' — کاین introduces the clause, کین = 'grudge/vengeance', and آن سی و هشت supplies the numeral 'those thirty‑eight' (آن = that/the). The trailing و is the joining particle used inside Persian numerals ('سی و هشت' = thirty‑eight).
segments.s388-g176: گرامی برادر = 'honored brothers' (noun phrase, grāmi = 'honored/valued' modifying 'brothers'). که اندر گذشت is a relative clause 'who passed/are gone' (گذشت = past of گذشتن 'to pass', here meaning 'died/left'); اندر is a classical preposition 'in/within' used idiomatically with گذشت to mean 'passed away.'
segments.s388-g241: همه دشت پا و بر و پشت بود is a nominal clause with the copula بود (past 'was') at the end. همه دشت (lit. 'the whole plain') places the quantifier adjective before the noun (typical Persian order). The coordinated list پا و بر و پشت names body-parts that characterize how the plain appeared; they function as a predicate nominal describing the scene, joined by و and completed by بود in the past tense.
segments.s388-g242: بریده سر و تیغ در مشت بود contains two coordinated noun phrases joined by و and a final copula بود. بریده modifies سر (an adjectival past participle: 'cut off' / 'severed') and precedes the noun as is normal in Persian poetry. تیغ در مشت is a locative phrase (تیغ 'sword' + در 'in' + مشت 'fist') meaning 'swords in (their) fists'; بود again is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s388-g243: سواران جنگی همی تاختند is a simple past clause with تاختند ('they charged/attacked') as the verb. سواران جنگی is a noun + postpositive adjective (common in older/poetic Persian) meaning 'military riders' or 'war-horsemen'. همی is an archaic/poetic adverb that accompanies past verbs to give an iterative or continuous/forceful sense; placed before the verb here it intensifies the action.
segments.s388-g244: به کالا گرفتن نپرداختند is a negated past-tense clause built with the prefixal negative ن- on پرداختند (they paid/attended). Here نپرداختند means 'they did not attend to / did not bother with.' The construction به + noun + infinitive (به کالا گرفتن) functions as the object/goal phrase 'to seize/take the goods' (کالا گرفتن = 'taking goods'), and the main verb states they did not do that.
segments.s388-g245: چو ترکان شنیدند کارجاسپ رفت is a temporal subordinate clause introduced by چو = 'when'. ترکان (Turks) is the subject, شنیدند is the past-tense verb 'they heard/learned', and کارجاسپ رفت is the reported content 'that Karjasp left' (a short reported clause placed after شنیدند). The sequence shows a classical Persian clause order: temporal introducer + subject + verb + reported clause.
segments.s388-g246: همی پوستشان بر تن از غم بکفت is a verbless/semiverbal resultive clause ending with بکفت (an archaic past form). همی again intensifies or marks the resultant state. پوستشان ('their skin') + بر تن ('upon the body') is a locative phrase, and از غم ('from grief') gives the cause. بکفت here describes the bodily reaction (an archaic intransitive past—'burst/shrank/trembled' in context), so the whole means roughly 'their skin (on) their bodies [was affected] from sorrow.'
segments.s388-g247: کسی را که بد باره بگریختند uses a relative clause with کسی را که ... بگریختند to mean 'whoever fled.' The particle را after کسی is an emphatic/definite marker used with an indefinite relative here; که introduces the relative clause. بد باره is an adverbial phrase 'on that ill-fated/ill-omened occasion' modifying the verb بگریختند (past plural 'they fled/escaped'). In older Persian poetry such relative constructions often mark an indefinite 'whoever'.
segments.s388-g248: دگر تیغ و جوشن فرو ریختند is a main clause whose subject is the coordinated noun phrase تیغ و جوشن ('swords and cuirasses/armor') and whose verb is the compound فرو ریختند (فرو- + ریختند 'they fell/poured down'). دگر here functions adverbially ('then' / 'again' / 'forthwith'), and فرو as a verbal prefix gives the downward/completely sense; ریختند is the past plural of ریختن meaning 'to pour' or 'to fall in a mass'—so the phrase means 'swords and armor rained/fell down.'
segments.s388-g33: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. لشکر is the subject ('the army/host'). بدانست is a past-tense verbal form meaning 'became known/realized' (literally 'it became known to them' or 'they learned'). کاسفندیار is the object/name brought by the verb: 'when the army learned that Esfandiyār...'.
segments.s388-g34: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from'. بند گران = 'heavy fetters/bonds' (بند = fetter, گران = heavy). رست is a classical perfective verb meaning 'was freed / escaped / was delivered'. و simply connects the two coordinated complements, and بد روزگار is a noun phrase 'ill fate / misfortune' describing what he escaped as well. The whole clause completes the content of 'they learned that...'.
segments.s388-g35: برفتند is a past plural verb 'they went / advanced'. یکسر here means 'all together / at once' (emphatic). گروها گروه is a rhetorical pairing 'bands and groups' (both words denote companies of warriors); together they form the subject/topic that performs the going.
segments.s388-g36: به پیش جهاندار = 'to/toward the presence of the sovereign' (به + پیش expresses motion toward someone's presence). بر تیغ کوه is a poetic locative: بر = 'on/upon', تیغ = 'point/edge' (metaphorical), کوه = 'mountain' — 'upon the mountain's ridge/edge'. This adverbial phrase gives the place toward which they advanced.
segments.s388-g37: بزرگان فزرانه = 'the great, wise ones' (an epithet: بزرگان 'great men' + فزرانه 'wise'). و introduces خویش اوی, 'his kinsmen' (خویش = kin; ش is the possessive element 'his', and اوی reinforces 'him/his' in classical style). Together this noun phrase is the subject of the following verb.
segments.s388-g38: نهادند is the past plural verb 'they placed / they laid'. سر بر زمین is a fixed accusative phrase 'head upon the ground' (i.e., prostrate). پیش اوی = 'before him / in his presence' (پیش = before/in presence of; اوی = him). The verb completes the action of the subject: 'they laid their heads on the ground before him.'
segments.s388-g39: چنین 'thus/so' sets the manner; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said/spoke' (classical phrasing), and نیک‌اختر اسفندیار is the speaker's name in apposition. The phrase introduces direct speech or an address that follows.
segments.s388-g40: که‌ای here functions as a vocative ('O you...') in older poetic register (the combination of که + ای). نامداران = 'renowned ones', and خنجرگزار = literally 'dagger-bearers' (an epithet for those who carry daggers or behave treacherously); together they form the direct address 'O renowned dagger-bearers.'
segments.s388-g41: همه (subject) + تیغ زهرآبگون (direct object) + برکشید (verb). برکشید is an epic/past 3rd-person-plural form of برکشیدن used here to mean “drew” (of a weapon). Word order is subject–object–verb, the normal classical Persian order, and زهرآبگون is a compound adjective modifying تیغ.
segments.s388-g42: یکایک is an adverb “one by one”; درآیید is another past 3rd-plural verb (درآوردن/درآمدن family) meaning “they came/entered/advanced”; و simply links the two actions; دشمن کشید uses کشید (past 3rd-plural of کشیدن) to indicate engaging/meeting or “struck/attacked” the enemy. The clause sequence describes successive actions.
segments.s388-g43: بزرگان is the plural subject “the great ones/elders”; برو here is an idiomatic/interjectory element preceding خواندند (its precise force is emphatic or archaic and does not carry its simple modern sense “go”); خواندند is past 3rd-plural “they called/cried”; آفرین is the exclamation “Bravo!” or acclamation being uttered.
segments.s388-g44: که introduces the content of their cry (“that …”). ما را uses را as a marker placing ‘us’ as the beneficiary/affected party; توی functions as the preposition “in/among” (here indicating inclusion/role) introducing the nouns افسر و تیغ کین, where افسر “helmet” and تیغ کین “sword of vengeance” are predicative/nominal complements — the copula (‘are’) is omitted (elliptical), which is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s388-g45: همه پیش تو is an adverbial phrase “all (of us) in your presence / before you.” جان گروگان کنیم is an idiomatic expression: گروگان here means a pledge/hostage and کنیم is the 1st-person-plural subjunctive/hortative form of کردن used to express resolve or intention (“let us/we will make/offer”). The clause means “we pledge our lives” (literally ‘make life a hostage’).
segments.s388-g46: به دیدار تو is a prepositional phrase “for/as the sight/meeting of you”; رامش جان is a nominal phrase meaning “the solace/comfort of the soul”; کنیم repeats the hortative/subjunctive verb ‘we make’ tying the parallel meaning to the previous clause: “we make seeing you the solace of our lives.” Word order is typical Persian: prepositional phrase + predicate.
segments.s388-g47: همه شب ‘all night’ is an adverbial time phrase; همی is an iterative/continuous adverb (in epic poetry it often emphasizes repeated or sustained action); لشکر is the direct object/collective “army”; آراستند is past 3rd-plural of آراستن meaning “they arrayed/arranged/prepared.” The clause describes a prolonged preparation through the night.
segments.s388-g48: همی again marks continuity; جوشن و تیغ are the coordinated objects “cuirass (armor) and sword”; پیراستند is past 3rd-plural of پیراستن/پیرآستن meaning “they adorned/armed/laid out” (here ‘prepared’ or ‘set in order’). The parallel structure links this concrete preparation (armor and swords) to the army’s general arraying in the previous clause.
segments.s388-g9: This is a nominal phrase introduced by an archaic/poetic conditional particle fragment at the start. 'گرزم' here is a fused/poetic form signalling a conditional or adversative sense (compare classical 'گر' = 'if'), followed by the demonstrative 'آن' ('that') and two coordinating epithets 'بداندیش بدخواه' modifying 'مرد' ('man'). Word order is demonstrative + adjectives + head noun (typical in poetry).
segments.s388-g10: 'دل من' is the possessed noun phrase 'my heart' (noun + possessive pronoun). 'ز' introduces cause/reason here ('because of' / 'on account of'), followed by 'فرزند خود' ('his own son' — خود reflexive/possessive) and the past verb 'تیره کرد' ('made dark'). The verb is simple past (3rd‑person singular) and takes the preceding clause as its subject/agent (the 'man' in the prior segment).
segments.s388-g11: Impersonal/gerundial construction: 'بد آید' ('evil/bad comes' or 'it is bad') states a general consequence. 'به مردم' marks who is affected ('to/for the people'), and 'ز کردار بد' gives the cause ('from/because of bad conduct'). The structure is causal statement: result (بد آید) + affected party + source/cause.
segments.s388-g12: Parallel clause repeating the impersonal verb 'بد آید' ('a bad thing/appearance arises'). 'به روی بد' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'on a bad face' or 'on the visage' (i.e., a bad expression appears), and 'از کار بد' again gives the cause ('from bad/deplorable deeds'). Note the poetic parallelism and repetition for emphasis.
segments.s388-g13: 'پذیرفتم' is first‑person singular past ('I accepted' / 'I acknowledge'). 'از کردگار جهان' is a prepositional phrase ('from the Creator of the world') marking source or authority. The clause functions as a main clause whose predicate is 'پذیرفتم'.
segments.s388-g14: 'شناسنده' is a substantive participle 'the knower' and serves as the thing accepted/acknowledged. The adjectives 'آشکار و نهان' ('manifest and hidden') follow and qualify what is known; word order is noun + modifiers. Grammatically this phrase supplies the content of what the speaker accepts from the Creator.
segments.s388-g15: 'که' introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'so that'); 'چون' here has a temporal/conditional sense ('when' / 'if') and is followed by the subject 'من' and the verb 'شوم' (1st‑person singular subjunctive/optative 'I become'). 'شاد و پیروزبخت' are predicative adjectives ('happy and fortunate') describing the condition of the speaker. The clause expresses a contingency ('if/when I become...').
segments.s388-g16: 'سپارم' is 1st‑person singular present/subjunctive ('I entrust' / 'I will entrust'). The object 'ترا' is split into the 2nd‑person suffix 'ت' (indexing 'you') plus the object marker 'را' that marks a definite direct object; it means 'to/unto you'. The list 'کشور و تاج و تخت' is the coordinated direct object ('the realm and crown and throne'). The verb expresses the speaker's promise to hand these over under the stated condition.
segments.s388-g153: تو گفتی is the 2nd-person past ('you said'). همه دشت is a noun phrase 'the whole/plain' with همه modifying دشت. بالای اوست is a predicate with بالای (literally 'above/upper') + او (he) + ست (copula 'is') — poetically inverted word order places the predicate after the reported clause, so the phrase means he stands above / towers over the entire plain.
segments.s388-g154: روانش is a possessed noun 'his spirit/soul' (روان + suffix -ش). همی is the archaic/prosodic progressive/continuative particle ('still/continually') common in epic style. در نگنجد is a present tense negative sense of گنجیدن ('to fit, be contained') with در marking the locative 'in/within' — together 'does not fit inside'. به پوست is a prepositional phrase ('into/within the skin') using به + پوست as the target; overall: 'his spirit still does not fit within his skin.'
segments.s388-g155: خروش آمد is a simple past clause 'a cry/roar came/rose up' (subject-noun then past verb). و links that clause to the following noun phrase. ناله کرنای is a juxtaposed genitive (typical in classical Persian) meaning 'the lament/cry of the trumpet' (ناله = lament, کرنای = trumpet/blast).
segments.s388-g156: برفتند is 3rd-person plural past ('they departed/moved off'). گردان لشکر is a compound noun phrase with گردان ('troop/division') qualifying لشکر ('army'), i.e. 'the army's divisions/regiments.' ز جای uses the poetic preposition ز = 'from' + جای 'place/position', yielding 'from their place/positions.' The verb precedes the noun phrase here for poetic emphasis but denotes the same past action.
segments.s388-g157: تو گفتی 'you said' (2nd-person past). ز خون is a prepositional/causal phrase 'from/because of blood' (ز = from/because of). بوم is 'the land/earth' and دریا شدست is an archaic perfect/past form (شدست = 'it became') meaning 'became a sea.' The clause is a poetic hyperbole: 'you said the land became a sea of blood.'
segments.s388-g158: ز خنجر again uses ز as 'from/because of' and خنجر is the instrument 'dagger.' هوا here means the 'air/sky' (not 'desire'), followed by چون 'like/as' and ثریا the Pleiades (a stellar image); شدست is the same archaic perfect 'became.' The structure: 'from daggers the sky became like the Pleiades' (a poetic simile).
segments.s388-g159: گران شد is past 'became heavy/weighty' used metaphorically ('became grave/formidable'). The noun phrase رکیب یل اسفندیار follows (رکیب = rival/competitor, یل = hero/brave man, اسفندیار = proper name) — poetic inversion places the verb before its subject for emphasis: 'Esfandiyar's rival became formidable.'
segments.s388-g160: بغرید is a past verb 'he/she/it roared/bellowed.' با is the instrumental preposition 'with,' and گرزه is the instrument 'mace.' گاوسار appears as a proper name/appellative at the end, so the clause reads 'Gaowsar roared with a mace' (subject supplied by the name). In epic diction subjects and instruments are often ordered for sound and emphasis.
segments.s388-g121: چو at the start is the temporal 'when'. گردوی جنگی is a compound descriptive phrase: جنگی is an adjective 'warlike' or 'of war' modifying گردوی; بر + میسره is a prepositional phrase: بر 'on/upon' governs میسره. The whole segment functions as a temporal locution ('when [the] war‑tumult/onset mounted upon the plain').
segments.s388-g122: بیامد is the past tense 'came'. The second چو again marks a temporal clause ('when'). خور is the noun 'sun' as subject; پیش برج بره is a prepositional phrase 'before the sign/tower of the Ram' where پیش = 'before/in front of' and برج = 'zodiac sign/tower' (بره = 'ram' = Aries). Word order places the verb before the temporal modifier, a poetic inversion.
segments.s388-g123: به پیش سپاه آمد is a motion clause: به پیش سپاه = 'to the front of the army/into the presence of the host' and آمد is the past 'came'; اسفندیار is the proper‑name subject coming into that place. The prepositional phrase precedes the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s388-g124: به زین اندرون is a locative phrase 'in/on the saddle' (به + زین + اندرون = 'within the saddle'). گرزه is the noun 'mace' and گاوسار follows as a proper name in apposition to the mace (i.e. the mace named Gausar). The clause reports what he bore on his saddle.
segments.s388-g125: به قلب اندرون is a prepositional phrase 'in the inner heart' (به = 'in/to', قلب = 'heart', اندرون = 'inner/within'). شاه گشتاسپ بود is a nominal clause with بود (past copula) 'was' linking شاه (king) and گشتاسپ (Gushtasp) — here identifying the king as Gushtasp located 'in the inner heart' (a poetic way to say Gushtasp was in the king's innermost thoughts).
segments.s388-g126: روانش is 'his soul/spirit' with the possessive suffix -ش. پر از is an adjectival phrase 'full of' taking از + noun; کین is 'hatred/vengeance' and لهراسپ is the object of that hatred (a proper name). بود is the past copula 'was' completing the predicate 'his soul was full of hatred (for) Lohrasp.'
segments.s388-g127: وزان is a fused poetic conjunctive phrase ('and from that' / 'because of that'). روی here refers to turn/face/appearance connected to the subsequent proper name: 'ارجاسپ صف برکشید' is the main clause meaning 'Arjasp drew up (برکشید) a صف (battle‑line)'. The verb برکشید is past tense 'raised/drew up'.
segments.s388-g128: ستاره is the noun 'star'. همی is an older/poetic adverb here meaning 'still/then' or emphasizing progressive aspect. روی دریا uses روی as a locative 'upon/on' followed by دریا 'sea'. ندید is the past negative 'did not see' (here rendering the impersonal sense 'no star was seen on the sea'). The phrase together states that no star could be seen above the sea.
segments.s388-g89: A noun phrase with postpositive modifiers: پسر (noun) 'son' is modified by two participial/adjectival words کوفته and سوخته, then followed by شهریار indicating possession ('the king's son'). In classical Persian the possessor can follow the noun, so read this as 'the king's son, (who is) crushed/exhausted and burned'.
segments.s388-g90: بیاری here means 'to help / aid' in the sense of a companion/helper (treated as 'aid' or 'come to help'); که introduces a relative clause 'who came'; آمد is past 'came'; جز is an exception marker 'except'; اسفندیار is the proper name. Together: 'no one came to help except Esfandiyar.'
segments.s388-g91: هم‌آورد is used as an adjectival/nominal concept 'an equal, a match'; او‌گر is the conditional particle 'if'; بیاید is subjunctive/potential 'should come'; منم is emphatic 'I too' (literally 'I am' but functioning as 'I also'). The clause reads 'If he comes as an equal, I too ...'.
segments.s388-g92: تن 'body' is the direct object; مرد here carries the nuance 'warrior/valiant man' (a descriptive noun); جنگی is an adjective 'warlike / fighter' attached to مرد; به + خاک 'into the dust' (به as motion/into) expresses the result; افگنم is a classical first-person verbal form (from افکندن) meaning 'I will cast / I would cast' — an optative/future-like 1sg. So 'I will cast the body of the warrior into the dust.'
segments.s388-g93: سپه را is the definite object with را marking 'the army' as object; همی is the habitual/continuous marker 'do/are doing' (presentative); دل‌شکسته is a compound adjective 'broken‑hearted / disheartened'; کنی is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive 'you make / you cause'. Together: 'You make the army disheartened.'
segments.s388-g94: به گفتار = 'by/with speech' (به used adverbially to mark means); بی‌جنگ 'without war / without fighting' qualifies the manner; خسته is the predicate adjective 'tired / weary'; کنی (again 2sg) 'you make'. The clause specifies the means: 'by words, without fighting, you cause them to be weary.'
segments.s388-g95: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; ارجاسپ is the subject name; بشنید is the simple past 3rd-person 'he heard'; گفتار اوی is 'his speech/words' with اوی as the possessive pronoun following the noun. Literal: 'When Arjasp heard his words...'
segments.s388-g96: بدید is past 'he saw' (3rd-person); آن is demonstrative 'that/the'; دل و رای = 'heart and counsel/mind' (two related internal faculties); هشیار is an adjective 'alert/awake/sane' modifying both nouns; اوی is the possessive 'his'. So: 'he saw that his heart and counsels were alert.'
segments.s388-g249: به is the directional preposition 'to/toward'. زنهار here is used as an interjection or noun 'znehâr' = 'beware/warning' (poetic vocative), and اسفندیار is the proper name (the addressee). آمدند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they came'. Together the clause means 'they came to Esfandiyar, (crying) “beware!”' — the interjection is placed after the preposition as a manner/utterance accompanying the motion.
segments.s388-g250: همه + دیده is a noun phrase 'all eyes' (همه = everyone/all modifying دیده = eyes); there is no ezâfe — a common poetic compression. چون introduces a simile 'like/as', جویبار is the simile-image 'a brook/stream', and آمدند is the plural past verb 'they came' agreeing with همه; literally 'all eyes came like a stream' (i.e. all eyes streamed or turned like a flowing brook).
segments.s388-g251: بریشان is a contracted/prepositional cluster (بر + ایشان) meaning 'to/for them' (poetic cliticization common in classical Persian). ببخشود is a past tense verb here meaning 'he spared/granted' (classical -d ending), and زورآزمای is a noun 'the strong-man / strength-tester' (the challenger). The clause means roughly 'he granted/spared the strongman to/for them.'
segments.s388-g252: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'from that time on / thereafter' (ازان = 'from that', پس = 'then/after'), نیفگند is the negative past 'did not throw/cast', کس را is the object 'anyone' with the definite/object marker را, and ز پای literally 'from the foot' (poetic idiom = 'to the ground' or 'off their feet'). The clause therefore means 'thereafter he did not cast anyone to the ground.'
segments.s388-g253: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. خون is 'blood'. نیا is used as a noun 'ancestor' (nīyā) placed before دل without an ezâfe — a poetic apposition meaning 'the ancestor's heart' or 'the ancestral heart'. بی‌آزار is an adjective 'harmless/without hurt', and کرد is the past verb 'he made/did'. The construction means 'from/of blood he made the (ancestral) heart harmless' (i.e. he caused the heart to be free from harm).
segments.s388-g254: سری را is the object 'the head' with the object marker را. بریشان again = بر + ایشان 'for/to them'. نگهدار is a noun/adjective 'guardian, preserver' used predicatively with کرد (i.e. 'made [it] a guardian'). کرد is the past verb 'made/did'. Grammatically this is a resultative: 'he made the head a guardian for them' (kept it as their guardian).
segments.s388-g255: خود 'khud' here means 'himself' (emphatic reflexive / the person himself) and is joined by و 'and' to لشکر 'the army/host'. آمد is the past 'came' (notice singular آمد used after a coordinated subject — classical Persian sometimes uses a singular verb for a compound subject to emphasize unity or because the nearest element controls agreement). به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'near the king' (به + نزدیک + شاه).
segments.s388-g256: پر از خون is a fixed phrase 'full of / covered with blood' (پر = 'full (of)', followed by از = 'of/from' + خون = 'blood'). بر is the preposition 'on/upon' (here used poetically to mean 'on them' or 'upon their bodies'), followed by the coordination و تیغ و رومی کلاه 'and sword and Roman (rūmī) hat/helmet'. The sequence lists the accoutrements covered/tinged with blood: sword and Roman helmet. The nouns are linked by و as a simple coordinate list.
segments.s388-g209: کنون is an adverb fronting the clause ('now'). تا introduces a subordinate/purpose clause here (classical Persian use: 'so that/let it be that'). کرا is an archaic interrogative/relative meaning 'who/whom' (object). بد appears here split from the verb (orthographic/metrical separation) and functions with دهد as the verbal idea 'give' (so treat بد + دهد as the verb 'بدهد' in older spelling). دهد is the verb in the present/subjunctive sense 'give/grant'. کردگار is the subject 'the Creator/God'.
segments.s388-g210: که introduces the content/result clause ('that'). پیروز is an adjective/predicate 'victorious'. گردد is the classical subjunctive/optative of گشتن used here 'become / may become'. از + ین = 'from this' (از = from; ین is the orthographic variant of این). کارزار is the noun 'battle' governing the source phrase 'from this battle'.
segments.s388-g211: وزان = 'from that / and from that' (connective adverb referring back). جایگه is the place/position. شد is the past/copular verb here meaning 'went / became' (classical use where شد marks movement/state). به is the preposition 'to/toward'. آوردگاه is 'battlefield' (destination). The order reflects classical Persian: adverbial phrase + noun + شد + prepositional destination.
segments.s388-g212: به جنگ اندر آورد is a verbal phrase 'brought into the battle': به + جنگ sets the direction/topic ('into the fight'), اندر is an archaic locative intensifier 'in/into', آورد is the past stem 'brought', یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/at once' modifying آورد, and سپاه is the object 'the army/host'.
segments.s388-g213: برانگیختند is a past plural verb 'they aroused/raised/kindled'. آتش is the direct object 'fire' used metaphorically here (the 'fire' of conflict). کارزار is a genitive or attributive noun 'of battle' (آتشِ کارزار = 'the fire of battle').
segments.s388-g214: هوا is the subject 'the air/atmosphere'. تیره گون is a compound/adjectival phrase ('dark-colored' / 'grew dark') formed by adjective تیره + modifier گون. شد is the copula 'became'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of'. گرد here means 'dust' (the dust raised by movement). سوار is the genitive/agent 'of the riders' (horsemen). The whole phrase: 'the air became dark from the riders' dust.'
segments.s388-g215: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. ارجاسپ is the proper-name subject. پیکار is the object 'battle' (what he saw). زان‌گونه is an adverb 'in that manner/thus' modifying دید. دید is the past verb 'saw'. The clause order is classical: 'When Arjasp saw the battle thus...'.
segments.s388-g216: ز is a causal preposition 'from/because of'. غم is 'sorrow/grief' giving the cause. پست گشت is a compound predicate 'became low / humbled' (پست + گشت(=گشت) = 'became low'). و is the conjunction 'and'. دلش is 'his heart' (دل + -ش, his). بردمید is a past transitive 'carried off / seized' used idiomatically 'his heart was carried away' = 'he was overcome'.
segments.s388-g177: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. ارجاسپ is the proper name (subject). آن is a demonstrative pronoun 'that / him' referring back to someone in the context. دید is the past verb 'saw / beheld' (چو + past = 'when he saw'). با + گرگسار is an instrumental/qualifying phrase 'with the wolf‑like (one)' or 'the wolf‑like in company', where گرگسار is an epithet 'wolf‑like / ferocious' placed after با.
segments.s388-g178: چنین modifies گفت and means 'thus / in this way'. گفت here is the main past verb 'said' (note: the provided sense list did not include the verbal sense 'say', so index set below is -1). کز is the fused poetic form of 'که از' = 'that / from', introducing the content clause that follows. لشکر بی‌شمار is a noun phrase 'the countless army/host' which is the beginning of that content clause.
segments.s388-g179: همه کشته شد is a passive/impersonal statement 'all were killed' (کشته = past participle 'killed', شد as auxiliary). هرک is the archaic/poetic contraction of هر که 'whoever'; جنگی is an appositive noun 'a warrior / fighter' describing that indefinite person. بدند is an archaic plural verbal form functioning here with the indefinite 'هرک' (see following segment for the locative action).
segments.s388-g180: به پیش is a directional prepositional group 'to/toward the front'; صف‌اندر is a fused poetic locative 'into the ranks' (در + صف fused as صف‌اندر). درنگی is a noun 'a pause / hesitation' (here part of a perfunctory idiom describing activity at the front), and the clause closes with بدند, the same archaic plural verb form that links back to هرک and states what happened to those fighters when they went forward.
segments.s388-g181: ندانم is 'I do not know' (ند + انم = negative + 'I know'); تو is the vocative/subject 'you'. خامش is an adjectival/nominal predicate 'silent' placed after the subject in poetic order. چرا مانده‌ای is an interrogative clause 'why have you remained / why are you staying' with مانده‌ای (present perfect / present result) addressing the addressee.
segments.s388-g182: چنین داستانها is the noun phrase 'such tales / such stories' (چنین = 'such'). چرا introduces the question 'why', and رانده‌ای is second‑person perfect/past participle + present 'you have driven away' (راندن in the perfect sense), asking why the addressee has 'driven away' or rejected such tales.
segments.s388-g183: ز گفتار او is a causal/prepositional phrase 'because of / from his speech' (ز = poetic 'from / because of'). تیز شد is a verbal phrase 'became sharp/keen / grew eager' (تیز here rendered 'keen/eager'), and the noun گرگسار follows as the subject or strong epithet 'the wolf‑like / ferocious one' — poetic inversion places the predicate before the noun.
segments.s388-g184: بیامد is the simple past 'came'. به پیش صف کارزار is a destination phrase 'to the front of the line of battle' where صف کارزار is a compound 'battle‑line / front of the battlefield' and به پیش marks motion toward that position.
segments.s388-g57: به ره (prepositional phrase) = literally 'to/on the road' (به + ره), placed before the verb as is common in poetry. بر فراوان طلایه is a compact noun phrase where بر (here functioning as a prepositional/relational element) + فراوان (adjective 'numerous') modifies طلایه 'vanguard/forefront'. بکشت is an archaic past-formal verb meaning 'he slew/caused to be killed' (the prefixed ب- and past ending indicate an older/perfect action). Word order is topical (place + object + verb).
segments.s388-g58: کسی کو نشد is an idiomatic, slightly archaic clause meaning 'there was no one who…' — کسی 'anyone/no one' plus the old relative/particle کو. نشد here functions as a negative/past auxiliary 'was not / did not'. کشته بنمود is a compound predicate: کشته 'killed' (the state) plus بنمود 'showed/made appear' — together implying 'appeared/made to show himself as killed' or 'turned and was seen as slain'. پشت ('back') is the body-part object of بنمود, i.e. 'show his back' (turned away).
segments.s388-g59: غمی گشت is a short verbal clause meaning 'grief came/it became grief' (گشت = became). و links the clauses. پرمایگان را بخواند uses را as the object/topic marker: پرمایگان 'the nobles/great ones' + را + بخواند, where بخواند here means 'called/summoned' (sense 'to call'). The verb is past/perfect narrative style.
segments.s388-g60: بسی پیش کهرم سخنها براند: بسی functions adverbially ('much'/'many'), پیش 'before/in the presence of', کهرم here refers to the venerable/old (an elderly person or august presence). سخنها 'words/speeches' is the direct object and براند (from راند) literally 'drove/put forth' — used poetically for 'uttered/put forward' many words. The clause thus means 'he uttered many words before the elder(s).' Note the poetic verb choice instead of a plain 'said'.
segments.s388-g61: که at the start introduces reported content: 'that'. ما را is a topical/dative marking ('for us' / 'we (as topic)'). جزین ('except this') restricts the content: ما را جزین بود در جنگ رای = 'for us there was no plan in the war except this' — بود is the past copula 'was'. رای here means 'plan/counsel' rather than simple 'opinion'. Word order is formal/poetic (topic + exception + copula + locative phrase).
segments.s388-g62: بدانگه 'thereupon' signals the temporal consequence; که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/when'. لشکر بیامد ز جای is a straightforward narrative past: لشکر 'the army/host' + بیامد 'came' + ز جای 'from its place/position'. The clause describes the army's movement immediately following the counsel.
segments.s388-g63: همی گفتم is a progressive/iterative past: همی 'continually/still' + گفتم 'I said/I was saying' (here reported speech). آن دیو را‌گر is an inverted/inlined conditional: را‌گر = 'if' (archaic/poetic), with آن دیو 'that demon' as the conditional object. به بند places the goal/state 'into bonds' after the conditional particle. The clause functions as the protasis ('if' clause) introduced inside reported speech.
segments.s388-g64: بیابیم is a subjunctive/optative form 'we may find/if we can obtain/capture' (here 'if we can secure'). گیتی شود is a simple future/result clause: گیتی 'the world' + شود 'will become' or 'will be'. بی‌گزند is a predicate adjective 'unharmed/safe'. Together: 'if we can bind him, the world will be safe.'
segments.s388-g49: This pre-verbal noun phrase identifies the subject and companions: پدر (the father) is the subject, followed by نیز 'also' (marking addition) and با + name 'with Farrokh Esfandiyar' (با indicates accompaniment). فرخ اسفندیار here functions as a proper epithet + name.
segments.s388-g50: همی is a frequent epic marker indicating continuous or iterative past action ('kept on / did') here functioning as an adverbial flavour; راز گفت is literally 'said/revealed a secret' (راز = secret, گفت is past of گفتن 'said' used here as verb). از introduces the topic 'about/concerning' (از بد روزگار = 'about the bad times' / 'of ill fate').
segments.s388-g51: ز (poetic variant of از) marks origin or source 'from'; خون جوانان 'the blood of the youths' is a genitive; پرخاشجوی is an adjective 'quarrelsome/aggressive' modifying جوانان. The whole phrase functions as a source/attribute for what follows.
segments.s388-g52: به رخ بر نهاد is a verbal clause: به رخ 'onto the face/cheek' (به = toward/on), بر is the prepositional particle 'upon', and نهاد is the past of نهادن 'placed/put' (here 'set/laid'). از دو دیده دو جوی is a descriptive phrase 'two streams from two eyes' (از = from, دیده = eyes, جوی = rivulet/stream) describing what was set on the cheek (i.e., tears or blood streams).
segments.s388-g53: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('for/that'), بودند is past plural copula 'they were', and کشته is a past participle 'slain' used predicatively. بران رزمگاه is a locative phrase 'on that battlefield' (بران = 'on/thereon', رزمگاه = battlefield).
segments.s388-g54: This string describes the condition of the slain: به سر 'on the head', بر reinforcing 'upon', ز خون و ز آهن 'with/from blood and with/from iron' (ز marks association/material or stain), and کلاه 'helmet' as the headgear. Together: 'helmets upon their heads, (marked) by blood and iron.' Note آهن in poetic context often evokes 'iron/steel' (weapons).
segments.s388-g55: همان شب 'that very night' (همان = the same/that), خبر 'news' is the topic, نزد here means 'to/at the presence of' (نزد ارجاسپ = in front of / reached Arjasp), and شد is a past/resultative verb 'happened / came to be' indicating the news arrived or was received.
segments.s388-g56: که introduces the content-clause ('that'), فرزند here means 'the son/child', نزدیک is adjectival/adverbial 'near/approaching', گشتاسپ is the proper name 'Gushtasp' used as possessor (the son of Gushtasp), and شد is the past copula/verb 'became/was' ('had become/was near'). The clause reports the news: that Gushtasp's son was near.
segments.s388-g65: بگیرم is a first‑person present/subjunctive form of گرفتن used here as a volitive/conditional ('if I seize' / 'let me seize'); the verb comes first (verb–object order). سر گاه ایران‌زمین is a noun cluster (سر 'head/chief' + گاه 'seat/occasion' + ایران‌زمین 'land of Iran') serving as the object/goal of بگیرم — literally 'the chief seat/place of the Iranian realm.'
segments.s388-g66: به هر مرز introduces the locus ('at/to every border'); بر ما کنند آفرین is a result clause: بر = 'upon', ما = 'us', کنند = third‑person plural present/subjunctive 'they will do/make' and آفرین is a noun exclamation 'praise/bravo' (object of کنند). Word order puts the place phrase first, then the verb phrase describing what people will do there.
segments.s388-g67: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. چون here means 'now that / when' (introducing a subordinate temporal/reason clause). گشاده شد is a past perfect/simple past passive/uartive: گشاده 'open' + شد 'became/was', so 'has been opened/freed.' آن دیوزاد is the demonstrative + noun 'that demon‑born.'
segments.s388-g68: به introduces a circumstance/means ('in/by/under'), followed by چنگست, a compressed poetic adjective/phrase describing manner or condition; ما را uses را as the clitic marking the affected party ('to us'), غم و سرد are coordinated predicates 'sorrow and cold', and باد is the optative particle 'may' (classical Persian wish formula). Together this yields the optative 'may sorrow and cold be ours (in his grasp).' The clause is a wish dependent on the previous temporal clause.
segments.s388-g69: ز ترکان is a genitive/partitive 'from/among the Turks.' کسی نیست is the existential negative 'there is no one.' همتای اوی is a noun phrase 'his equal / counterpart' (همتا = equal, اوی = his/her/of him), completing the idea 'There is no one among the Turks equal to him.'
segments.s388-g70: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that.' گیرد is the present/subjunctive of گرفتن and here has a potential/ability sense ('who could take' / 'who would take'). به رزم = 'in battle,' اندرون جای اوی = 'the inner place/position of him' (اندرون 'inside/inner,' جای 'place/position,' اوی 'his'), so the whole relative clause says 'who could take his place in the inner battle.'
segments.s388-g71: کنون 'now' begins the clause. با دلی شاد 'with a joyful heart' uses با + an adnominal noun phrase (دلی 'heart‑like / wholehearted' + شاد 'joyful'). و پیروز بخت links 'and victorious/favored fate' — پیروز modifies بخت; word order places modifiers after the head in this poetic register.
segments.s388-g72: به توران 'toward Turan' (به + place). خرامیم is first‑person plural present/indicative (or hortative) of خرامیدن 'to stride/prance proudly' — here 'we stride/proudly march.' با تاج و تخت is a coordinated instrument/possession phrase 'with crown and throne.' The clause as a whole states their proud advance toward Turan bearing sovereignty.
segments.s388-g225: غمی شد is a compact poetic clause meaning “a sorrow/grief occurred.” شد is used here as a past event verb (“happened/occurred”). در ارجاسپ را places the affected person (ارجاسپ) in an oblique locative construction—در + name + را—so the phrase means “a grief happened in/for Arjasp” with را marking the definite/affected patient. زان شگفت is a causal/origin phrase “from/for that wonder,” zān (زان) pointing back to the prior marvel.
segments.s388-g226: هیون خواست and راه بیابان گرفت form two coordinated actions. خواست is the simple past of خواستن meaning “desired/was moved” (here idiomatically “were set in motion / took fright”), and راه بیابان گرفت is a light-verb construction where گرفت (“took”) governs راه بیابان (“the desert road”) meaning “took to the desert.” The conjunction و links the two verbs into a single sequence of reaction.
segments.s388-g227: خود و ویژگان is a coordinated subject meaning “he himself and the distinguished/companions.” بر هیونان is a prepositional phrase “upon/against the beasts,” and مست here is figurative (“intoxicated/frenzied”) describing the beasts or the state of the assault; in context it conveys a furious, roused condition. Word order is emphatic/poetic: subject + prepositional complement + adjective.
segments.s388-g228: برفتند is the main past verb “they advanced/went forth.” The second coordinated clause, اسپان گرفته به دست, uses the participial adjective گرفته (“having seized”) with اسپان (“horses”) as its object and به دست as an adverbial phrase “into/into the hand / in hand.” Grammatically this is a circumstantial participial phrase modifying the subjects: “and (having) seized the horses in hand.”
segments.s388-g229: سپه را places the army as a definite/affected noun with the object marker را. بران رزمگه is a locative phrase “on/thereupon that battlefield” (بران = ‘thereupon/on that’), and بر بماند uses بماند (a third-person narrative form) meaning “remained/was left.” Together the clause says the army was left/stayed upon that battlefield—بماند functioning as narrative past/optative in epic usage.
segments.s388-g230: خود و مهتران is the coordinated subject “he himself and the chiefs.” سوی is the directional preposition “toward,” خلخ is the place-name or goal (here a proper/place word in the poem), and براند is the verb “drove/rode” (drive/lead). The clause therefore means “he and the chiefs rode off toward Khalkh.” Note: مهتران is an honorific plural (‘the great/chiefs’) and خلخ functions as the destination; if the dictionary senses do not list a place sense for خلخ, treat it as a toponym in context.
segments.s388-g231: خروشی برآمد is a noun + past verb construction meaning “a roar/cry issued forth / arose.” ز اسفندیار marks the source/agent (“from Esfandiyar”). This is a common poetic order (noun + برآمد) to announce a sound or event coming from someone.
segments.s388-g232: بلرزید is the past verb “trembled/quake(d).” ز آواز او is a causal/source phrase “from/by his voice.” کوه و غار are the affected plural nouns “mountain and cave.” Note the poetic word order with verb early and subject(s) after; also in epic poetry the final -ند plural ending is often dropped from past verbs, so a plural subject (کوه و غار) can appear with the verb form بلرزید without an explicit -ند.
segments.s388-g105: هم اندر زمان is an adverbial phrase placed up front: هم (also/at the same time) + اندر زمان (in that time). The core clause is لشکر او را سپرد where لشکر (army) is the object, او را is the definite/object-marked indirect object ('to him'), and سپرد is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular verb meaning 'entrusted/handed over.'
segments.s388-g106: کسانی که بودند ... is a relative clause modifying an implicit antecedent: کسانی 'those who/people who' + که (relative 'who') + بودند (past copula 'they were') followed by two adjectival predicates joined by و. The structure is simple past descriptive: 'those who were X and Y.'
segments.s388-g107: همه شب همی is a habitual/time expression ('every night, continually'): همه شب 'every night' and همی as an iterative/habitual particle. خلعت is the direct object 'robe(s) of honour' and آراستند is a past, 3rd-person plural verb 'they adorned/donned.' Word order places the time phrase first, object before the verb, and the verb at the end.
segments.s388-g108: همی باره is an adverbial combination giving repetition/iteration ('again and again' / 'on repeated occasions'). پهلوان is the object 'champion/hero' and خواستند is past 3rd-person plural 'they sought/wanted.' The verb-final order is standard; the particles signal repeated action.
segments.s388-g109: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause ('when'). خورشید زرین is a noun + epithet 'the golden sun' and سپر is the object of برگرفت, where برگرفت is a past, 3rd-person singular verb meaning 'took up/raised.' The clause reads literally 'When the golden sun took up (its) shield.'
segments.s388-g110: شب تیره is the subject 'the dark night.' In classical Persian ز + او (here written as ز / و) functions as از او 'from him/over him' (the two parts together mean 'from him'). دست بر سر گرفت is idiomatic: گرفت (past 'seized/took') with the phrase دست بر سر 'laid a hand on [his] head', i.e. 'overtook/overwhelmed him.' The verb is past 3rd-person singular and the prepositional phrase بر سر marks the locus ('on the head').
segments.s388-g111: بینداخت is a past transitive verb 'he threw on / he put on.' پیراهن مشک رنگ is the object noun phrase where پیراهن 'tunic/robe' is qualified by مشک رنگ 'musk-coloured' (adjective phrase follows the noun, as is common in Persian). The whole means 'he donned a musk‑scented/‑coloured tunic.'
segments.s388-g112: چو introduces a comparison/temporal clause 'when/as.' یاقوت شد is 'became like a ruby' (شد past 'became'). مهر چهرش is a poetic compound meaning the radiance/beauty (here: glow/blush) of his face (چهرش = his face), with به رنگ indicating the resulting colour 'in the colour of.' The clause therefore says the glow of his face turned ruby-red.
segments.s388-g25: بدان (here written as ب + دان) is an archaic/directional element meaning 'to that' or 'thereupon'; مرد بد گوی is a noun phrase 'man of evil speech' (بد modifies گوی 'speaker/saying'), and گریان شدم is a periphrastic past: the adjective/participle گریان 'weeping' plus the auxiliary شدم 'I became' = 'I wept / I was made to weep.' Word order is topical/poetic (circumstance → subject phrase → predicate).
segments.s388-g26: ز introduces the cause ('because of'); درد دل شاه is a genitive/possessive sequence 'the pain (dard) of the king's heart' (دل = 'heart'); بریان شدم is metaphorical: بریان 'roasted/consumed' + شدم 'I became' = 'I was consumed/tormented' by that sorrow. The clause expresses resultive state caused by the king's grief.
segments.s388-g27: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now'; آنچ is a relative 'that which' (i.e. what was); بد بود = 'was bad' (بود is past copula); از ما گذشت means 'passed from us' or 'is past for us' (from + pronoun + گذشت 'passed'). The clause states that the earlier ill thing has now passed away from the speaker's party.
segments.s388-g28: غم رفته is a noun + participle 'the sorrow that has gone/left'; نزدیک ما = 'near to us' (نزدیک before the pronoun = 'near us'); بادگشت here is an archaic/poetic noun/verb-form meaning 'reversal/return' (optative/hopeful sense 'may it be reversed / may it return'). The line expresses a wish for the gone sorrow to be turned/returned near the speaker.
segments.s388-g29: ازین پس ('از' + variant 'این' = 'from now on') is a temporal phrase; چو = 'when/if' (temporal/conditional); من تیغ را برکشم places the object تیغ with the object marker را before the verb برکشم 'I draw/lift' (classical poetic verb); overall: 'when I draw my sword.' Note that برکشم is a present/subjunctive form used to express a future/occurrent action in poetry.
segments.s388-g30: وزین here is best read as the conjunction + demonstrative 'و زین' ('and from this/these') in poetic spelling; کوه‌پایه 'foothill(s)' names the place; سراندر کشم is a verb phrase where سراندر (lit. 'head-in / heads-into' in older compounding) and کشم (poetic form of 'to pull/drag/strike') express the action 'I will drag/pull (them) from the foothills.' The clause continues the conditional action set by چو ... برکشم.
segments.s388-g31: نه ... نه introduces correlative negation. ارجاسپ مانم literally 'I remain Arjasp' but here means 'I am not Arjasp' (مانم, from ماندن, is used poetically where modern Persian would use هستم); نه خاقان چین = 'nor the khagan of China.' The structure lists identities the speaker denies.
segments.s388-g32: Continues the negated list: نه کهرم نه خلخ نه توران زمین = 'neither Kahrm nor Khalakh nor the land of Turan.' Each item is presented as something the speaker is not; زمین here is 'land/country' describing توران. The line is a catalogue-style denial of identifications.
segments.s388-g1: برآمد is a past tense verb 'rose/arose' placed at the start; بران is an adverbial/old-fashioned pointer ('thereupon, on that') and تند is an adverb 'swiftly'. بالا فراز is a compound adverbial phrase ('up to the high summit' or 'onto the height'), with بالا 'up/high' modifying فراز 'summit'. The whole segment gives a single past action: 'he rose quickly onto that high ridge.'
segments.s388-g2: چو here = 'when'; روی پدر is the direct object phrase 'the father's face' (روی = face). دید is the past 'he saw'. بردش نماز is idiomatic: برد (plus the object suffix ش = 'him') with نماز, literally 'prayer took him' — i.e. he was seized/overcome by prayer or began to pray. The clause links temporally to the prior rise: upon seeing his father's face he was moved to prayer.
segments.s388-g3: پدر داغ دل بود is a nominal predication: 'the father was (بود) with a burning/grieving heart' (داغ دل = grief). بر پای جست is a simple past action 'he sprang to his feet' (بر = onto, پای = foot/feet, جست = leapt). Word order places the state first then the sudden motion.
segments.s388-g4: ببوسید is the simple past 'he kissed (him)'. و links the coordinate verb بسترد, here meaning 'he covered/veiled' (بسترد + object رویش = 'covered his face'). رویش is روی + the 3rd‑person possessive suffix ش = 'his face'. به دست is an instrumental phrase 'with (his) hand' (به ... دست = by/with the hand).
segments.s388-g5: بدو is an indirect object phrase 'to him' (literally 'unto him'). گفت here functions as the speech verb 'said' (the lemma senses listed are conversational but context shows it is 'said'). یزدان سپاس is an exclamatory formula 'thanks be to God' and ای جوان is a vocative 'O young man' addressing the son.
segments.s388-g6: که introduces the content clause ('that' / 'for') and دیدم is 'I saw'. ترا is the object: ت (2nd‑person suffix) + را (object marker) = 'you' (accusative). شاد و روشن‌روان are predicative adjectives 'happy and bright‑souled/cheerful'. The clause reports what he sees about the addressee.
segments.s388-g7: ز من = 'toward me' (ز is a poetic 'from/toward'). در دل‌آزار و تندی is a compound nominal phrase: دل‌آزار 'heart‑hurting / causing grief' and تندی 'harshness / fierceness'. مدار is a negative imperative 'do not be' (here attached to the preceding qualities): together the clause says 'do not be toward me one who causes grief or is harsh.'
segments.s388-g8: به کین خواستن = 'in wanting/seeking vengeance' (به + کین + infinitive خواستن). هیچ کندی مدار is an emphatic negative: هیچ 'no/none', کندی 'slowness', مدار again the negative imperative 'do not be'. Together: 'do not show any slowness in seeking revenge.' The sentence therefore contrasts 'do not hurt me' with 'do not be slow to take vengeance.'
segments.s388-g137: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause; ارجاسپ is the subject (proper name); دید is the past verb 'saw' whose object follows as آن سپاه گران ('that heavy/ formidible host'). Word order here is flexible poetic Persian: 'چو + subject + verb + object'.
segments.s388-g138: This is a noun phrase in apposition to 'سپاه': گزیده سواران ('chosen horsemen') followed by نیزه‌وران ('spear‑wielders'), a coordinate list of the host's elite troops. گزیده is an adjective/participle placed before the plural noun سواران; نیزه‌وران is a plural agent noun (‑ان).
segments.s388-g139: بیامد is simple past 'came' (aorist). یکی here means 'a certain one / someone' as subject; تند is an adjective 'swift' modifying that person. بالا گزید is a compact poetic verbal phrase: گزیدن with بالا here means 'spring up / leap up' (classical poetic usage).
segments.s388-g140: به هر سوی لشکر is a directional phrase 'towards every side of the army'; همی بنگرید is the classical present/progressive third‑person form ('he looked / he kept looking'). همی + بنگرید gives the habitual/continuous nuance 'he cast his gaze about'.
segments.s388-g141: ازان پس is an adverbial 'after that / thereupon'; بفرمود is the honorific/polite past 'he commanded/said', here functioning as 'he ordered'. تا introduces the content clause of the order ('so that / that ...'), and ساروان is the addressee/agent 'the caravan‑leaders' (those who should carry out the order).
segments.s388-g142: هیون (beasts/pack‑animals) is the object to be brought; آورد is the verb 'bring' — here governed by تا after a command, so it functions like a subjunctive/imperative ('bring'); پیش کاروان means 'before / in front of the caravan' (پیش = in the presence/of/in front of).
segments.s388-g143: چنین 'thus' introduces reported speech; گفت here is the verb 'said' (the supplied sense list gave noun senses, so the exact verbal sense is the speech‑verb). با + نامداران marks the addressee 'with / to the renowned ones'. براز appears in the manuscript as a name or vocative here; the dictionary senses given ('manure') do not fit the context, so its sense is not among the provided options.
segments.s388-g144: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. این کار 'this affair / matter' is the subject; گردد is the literary/subjunctive/present form of 'گردیدن' meaning 'become / turn out / happen' (here 'will become / will prove to be'). به مابر is a dative/benefactive phrase 'for us / on our part'; دراز 'long' predicates the clause: 'that this matter will be long for us.'
segments.s388-g81: دلش is دل + enclitic possessive -ش (his), so literally ‘his heart/mind’. بود is the past copula here (‘was’). پربیم is a compound adjective formed with پر- ‘full (of)’, meaning ‘full of fear/ anxious’. The second clause repeats the pattern with سر (head) as the possessed noun and پر + شتاب ‘full of haste’ — typical Persian adjective phrases with پر following the noun.
segments.s388-g82: This is a compact poetic clause in which ازو (از او, ‘from him’) sets the experiencer; دور ‘far’ modifies the following predicate. بد here is an adverbial intensifier ‘badly/utterly’. خورد is the past of خوردن used idiomatically as ‘befell/struck’ or ‘came to pass’ so the phrase says that آرام و خواب (‘calm and sleep’) were driven/fled far from him (typical verb‑final idea condensed in poetry).
segments.s388-g83: یکی is the indefinite ‘a certain’. ترک names the ethnic/warrior identity (‘a Turk’) and is modified by the compound بد نام ‘ill‑named/ill‑reputed’ (bad + name = notorious). اون is a demonstrative ‘that’ pointing to the man, and گرگسار is an epithet (‘wolf‑like’/ferocious) attached after it — a typical poetic string of epithets after the noun.
segments.s388-g84: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’ (short form of از). لشکر is the collective ‘army/host’ (here the source). بیامد is the past tense ‘came’ (he came forward), and بر before شهریار works directionally ‘to/toward’ (approached the king). The subject (the Turk) is understood from the previous segment.
segments.s388-g85: بدو is به + او ‘to him’ (directional dative). گفت here introduces direct speech (‘he said’). کای is the vocative particle ‘O’ used to address a ruler; شاه ترکان is ‘king of the Turks’ (شاه modified by the genitive plural ترکان). چین in this line is an attributive/epithet whose precise historical/geographical shade can vary in poetry (it can read as an additional epithet or place‑name), so the phrase functions as a formal vocative addressing the king.
segments.s388-g86: به یک تن is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘on account of/for one person’ (یک تن = a single person). مزن is the negative imperative form ‘do not strike/ do not smite’ used here idiomatically with خویشتن (‘yourself’) to mean ‘do not cast/throw yourself (down)’. بر زمین is the goal ‘to the ground/onto the earth’. The clause is an admonition directed at the king: do not abase/throw yourself down because of one man.
segments.s388-g87: سپاهی here names the military force (collective ‘army’). همه (‘all/the whole’) intensifies the noun; خسته و کوفته are two adjectival predicates meaning ‘tired’ and ‘beaten/exhausted’. The sequence is a straightforward nominal+adjective description of the army’s state.
segments.s388-g88: This short clause lists consequences/conditions: گریزان ‘fleeing’ (present participial/adjectival), و ‘and’, بخت ‘fortune/fate’ with اندر ‘in’ (archaic preposition), and آشوفته ‘disturbed/confused’. Together they mean the army was fleeing and destiny/fortune was in disorder — typical Shahnameh compact coordination.
segments.s388-g161: بیفشارد is an archaic past action-form (third person) built with the old epic b- prefix + verb stem, here meaning 'smote/struck/crushed'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon' and governs the following noun. گرز پولاد is a noun phrase 'steel mace' (گرز = mace, پولاد = steel). مشت follows as the direct instrument/force 'a blow' (مشت here = 'blow' rather than literal fist). The word order is V + prep phrase + object instrument, typical of Persian poetic compression.
segments.s388-g162: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (introducing source or effect). قلب here means 'heart' (i.e. the chest/heart as target of a wound). سپه گرد is a compressed genitive/attributive phrase referring to the host/army (گرد in epic usage helps form the martial noun 'troop/host'); the phrase as a whole means 'of the host/army'. بکشت is an archaic past form (b- + کشت) meaning 'he killed'. The clause order is source (ز قلب) + genitive (سپه گرد) + verb.
segments.s388-g163: چنین گفت is the formula 'thus said' (چنین = thus; گفت here functions as 'said' in the narrative idiom). کز introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'that / because', and کین 'hatred/vengeance' names the motive. فرشیدورد is a proper name (the object of the hatred). The whole segment introduces the content of what was said.
segments.s388-g164: ز دریا is a prepositional phrase 'from the sea'. برانگیزم is a first-person-present/future-oriented form 'I will rouse/raise' (the -م ending shows 1st person; in epic narration direct speech often preserves this form). امروز 'today' gives temporal specification. گرد at the end is the martial/epic noun referring to the assembled force/host to be raised (here 'a host'). The clause reads as a first-person declaration of intent.
segments.s388-g165: ازان پس is a temporal sequence 'after that/thereupon'. سوی میمنه means 'toward the right (side)', with میمنه = right flank/side. حمله برد is an idiomatic verb phrase 'he carried out an attack/launched an assault' (برد here participates in an older compound sense 'to carry/execute' in the poetic register). Word order places the direction before the action, as is common in Persian.
segments.s388-g166: عنان is 'the reins/bridle' (literal object). باره here is a bound poetic element meaning a load/turn/occasion (it helps form the phrase framing what is handed). تیزتگ is a proper name/nickname (likely of a horse or swift mount) and is marked as definite with را, the Persian direct-object marker. سپرد is the past-tense verb 'he entrusted/handed over'. The phrase literally: 'he handed the reins to Tiztag.'
segments.s388-g167: صد و شست is the numeric phrase 'one hundred and sixty' (صد = one hundred; شست = sixty). گرد again is the epic noun indicating a body or group (here 'of the host/round of fighters'). از دلیران is the genitive 'of the brave (men)'. بکشت is the archaic past 'he killed' (b- + کشت). The clause thus gives the casualty count and agent.
segments.s388-g168: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. کهرم in context is a personal reference ('Kahram' / the veteran) — in epic Persian proper names or epithets can appear in forms that look like common nouns. چنان 'thus/so' refers back to the preceding scene. دید is simple past 'saw', and بنمود is a past causative/attested poetic verb 'showed/revealed' (here 'showed his back' = 'turned away/fled'). پشت is 'back'. The clause describes Kahram's reaction on seeing what had happened.
segments.s388-g129: ز بس is a short causal/quantitative phrase: ز (a poetic preposition) + بس (‘so much’/‘enough’) = 'because of so many'. It is followed by a simple coordination of nouns (نیزه و تیغهای بنفش) — 'spears and purple swords' — with و joining them; word order is conventional noun coordination with the color adjective بنفش postposed to تیغهای.
segments.s388-g130: هوا گشته is a verb‑predicate phrase: هوا (the air/atmosphere) + گشته (perfect/preterite sense 'has become'); here گشته = 'became'. پر is used in the predicative sense 'full (of)', followed by the adjective/compound modifier پرنیانی 'silken' modifying what fills the air, and درفش (banner) names the item related to those silken plumes. The line uses compact, poetic ordering: subject (هوا) + verb (گشته) + predicative complement.
segments.s388-g131: بشد is used narratively as 'became'/'turned' (past/result), and it precedes its subject in poetic inversion: بشد + قلب ارجاسپ = 'Arjasp's heart became...'. چون آبنوس is a simile ('like/as ebony') modifying قلب; چون introduces the comparison and follows the verb–subject unit.
segments.s388-g132: سوی راستش means 'toward/on his right' with سوی marking direction and راستش ('his right') the target; what follows is a coordinated list of instruments/noises (کهرم و بوق و کوس). و simply joins the items. کهرم here is an archaic or specialized word (see note) listed with other battle‑instruments; the phrase describes what is placed/on his right.
segments.s388-g133: سوی marks direction 'toward'. میسره appears here as a proper name/place or epithet and is followed by نام ('named') plus شاه ('king') and چگل (a proper name or epithet); the whole phrase functions as a directional apposition: 'toward Misreh, named Shah Chagel.' Several items (میسره, چگل) are likely names/epithets rather than their dictionary senses.
segments.s388-g134: که introduces a relative clause ('who/whom that ...') and here links back to the named king; در جنگ = 'in battle' sets the circumstance. ازو خواستی is an SOV pattern where ازو ('from him/of him') + خواستی (past 'you asked/desired') means 'you asked of him', and شیر دل ('lion‑heart') is the object phrase (an epithet: 'a brave/lion‑hearted one') that completes the request.
segments.s388-g135: برآمد (past 'arose/issued forth') is the main verb, followed by ز هر دو سپه 'from both armies' (ز marking origin, هر دو 'both', سپه 'army/host'). گیر و دار here is a fixed, idiomatic pairing meaning a sort of 'struggle/retinue/engagement' and functions as a coordinated noun phrase with و; the line describes what rose from the two hosts. Some component senses are archaic/idiomatic and not exactly matched to the dictionary options.
segments.s388-g136: به پیش اندر آمد is a verbal phrase 'came forward/before them' (به پیش = 'to the front/before', اندر آمد = 'entered/came in'). گو before a name is an attributive/nominalizing or vocative particle meaning 'called/so‑named' (here introducing the name), so گو اسفندیار = 'Esfandiyar (so‑called)' — in effect the line states that Esfandiyar came forward.
