segments.s388-g273: بدو گفت is a light-periphrastic reporting: بدو (to him) + گفت (said, past 3rd sg). شاها is a vocative address 'O king'; تو is the explicit subject 'you' that the following predicate will refer to; از خون من is a prepositional phrase 'of/regarding my blood' (از + noun + possessive pronoun). Word order is typical for older Persian: reporting clause (he said to him) followed by direct address and subject phrase.
segments.s388-g274: ستایش نیابی is a negative present/subjunctive assertion addressed to تو earlier: ستایش 'praise' is the object; نیابی is the negative form (ن- + یابی) of یافتن in the 2nd-person form, here meaning 'you will not find / you do not find' (classical/polite future/present-subjunctive sense). به هر انجمن is an adverbial phrase 'in any assembly/gathering' (به + هر + انجمن).
segments.s388-g275: یکی بنده باشم is an optative/wish construction: یکی (a/once/some) + بنده (servant) + باشم (1st sg subjunctive of بودن, here 'may I be' / 'let me be'). بپیشت and بپای are archaic/contracted locative phrases (به + پیش/پا + 2nd-person clitic -ت): بپیشت = 'in your presence / before you', بپای = 'at your feet / abiding at your feet' (here بپای carries the sense 'abide/persist' rather than literal 'lie in wait').
segments.s388-g276: همیشه (always) modifies the following clause. به نیکی is a goal/directional phrase 'toward goodness' (به + noun). ترا را (here split as ت + را) marks the person affected: ت (2nd-person clitic) = 'you', را = definite/object marker; رهنمای is a nominal/agentive form 'guide' used predicatively (the verb 'be' is elliptical or implied from the prior wish), so the sequence means 'I will be/serve as your guide to goodness always.'
segments.s388-g277: به هر بد introduces a conditional/adverbial 'at/for every ill (that)...'. که آید is a relative clause 'that comes' (که + آید, 3rd-sg. of آمدن). زبونی is a noun 'humiliation/abjection'; کنم is 1st-sg. subjunctive/present of کردن used here to express willingness/promise 'I will make/do' → 'I will abase myself' or 'I will submit to humiliation' in the face of any misfortune. Word order places the conditional phrase before the clause expressing the speaker's action.
segments.s388-g278: به رویین دژت رهنمونی کنم is 'I will give counsel/ guidance regarding your iron fortress': به (for/regarding) + رویین (iron-hard, attributive adjective) + دژت (دژ 'fortress' + enclitic -ت 'your') + رهنمونی (guidance/counsel) + کنم (I will do/make). The verb کنم takes the noun 'رهنمونی' as its object: 'I will do/give guidance.'
segments.s388-g279: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that' / 'that'), leading into what was to be done. بند بر دست و پای is a noun phrase 'fetters/bonds upon (his) hands and feet' (بند 'fetters' + بر 'on/upon' + دست و پای 'hands and feet'). The whole chunk reads 'he ordered that (they put) fetters on his hands and feet.'
segments.s388-g280: ببردند is 3rd-plural past 'they took/brought (away)'. بازش here functions as a fused pronoun/clitic meaning 'him back / him again' (poetic contraction where باز + pronominal clitic refer to the person); به پرده سرای is the destination 'to the curtain-chamber/private apartment' (به + پرده 'curtain/veil' + سرای 'chamber/house'). Word order places the verb before its object and destination (typical in narrative.)
segments.s388-g201: دو دست = 'two hands' (numeral + noun) placed before the verb as the object. از پس پشت is a prepositional phrase meaning 'from behind the back' (از = from; پس here = 'after/behind'; پشت = 'the back [of the body]'). بستش is a past-tense transitive verb with the enclitic -ش = 'him/it', here meaning 'they bound him'; چو سنگ is a simile 'like a stone' modifying how the binding was done.
segments.s388-g202: گره زد is a light-verb construction 'to make/tie a knot' (گره = 'knot'; زد is the simple past of زدن used here as a light verb). به گردن = 'around/on the neck' (به = to/onto; گردن = neck). برش here functions as a pronominal/clitic element referring to 'him' (archaic poetic form), and پالهنگ (palāhang) is the noun placed after the verb (poetic inversion) serving as the subject 'the leopard'.
segments.s388-g203: به لشکرگه آوردش = 'brought him to the army-camp' (به + place noun = to; لشکرگه = army-camp; آوردش = brought + enclitic -ش 'him'). از پیش صف = 'from the front of the ranks' (از = from; پیش = 'before/in front of'; صف = the battle-line/rows). The verb آمد/آوردش governs the following participial phrase.
segments.s388-g204: کشان is a present/adverbial participle 'dragging' that describes how he was brought. و = 'and.' ز خون بر لب آورده کف is a compact clause: ز خون = 'from/of blood', بر لب آورده = 'brought to (his) lip' (آورده = 'had brought' or 'was brought'), and کف = 'foam' (i.e. foam of blood at the mouth). Word order is compact and typical of poetic phrasing.
segments.s388-g205: فرستاد = 'he sent' (simple past transitive). بدخواه را = 'the ill-wisher' marked with را as definite/direct object. نزد شاه = 'to the presence of the king' (نزد = 'near/in the presence of'; شاه = 'king'). The construction is a standard S + V + O + directional phrase.
segments.s388-g206: به دست = instrumental 'by the hand of / through' (به = by/through; دست = 'hand' used idiomatically for 'agency'). همایون is a titular/name used here for the agent (Homāyūn). زرین کلاه is a descriptive compound 'golden-capped' (زرین = golden; کلاه = cap/hat), appended to identify Homāyūn.
segments.s388-g207: چنین گفت = 'thus he said' (چنین = thus/so; گفت = 'said' — verb used in narrative). کاین را = 'this one' with را marking it as definite object. به پرده سرای = 'into the curtain-chamber' or 'into the inner/quarantined room' (به = into/to; پرده = curtain/veil; سرای = room/house — here a locative phrase indicating where to confine him). The imperative that follows applies to this object and location.
segments.s388-g208: ببند is an imperative 'shut/bind (him)' (second-person command). و = 'and' linking the two commands. به کشتن مکن = a negative injunction 'do not [bring] to killing' (به + کشتن = 'toward killing'; مکن = 'do not do'). هیچ رای = 'any counsel/plan' (هیچ = 'any/none' used to negate; رای = 'plan/opinion'), so the whole clause means 'do not devise any plan to kill him.'
segments.s388-g17: پرستش بهی برکنم = the direct object phrase + verb. پرستش بهی is a noun phrase ('worship/adoration' qualified by 'بهی' = 'excellent/fair'), and برکنم is a 1st-person singular present/subjunctive/predicative verb meaning 'I will remove / I will give up.' The phrase زین جهان is a prepositional-like phrase 'from this world' (z + in), where جهان means 'this life/the world'; note that زین is written together here in the poem but functions as the poetic preposition 'from this'.
segments.s388-g18: سپارم ترا تاج و تخت مهان = main verb clause. سپارم is a 1st-person singular verb 'I entrust / I give over'; ترا (written here in two pieces, ت + را) is the old-style object phrase 'to you' (ت = 2nd-person, را = object marker); تاج و تخت list the things entrusted ('crown and throne'). مهان is an epithet modifying the crown/throne ('of the great' / 'of the mighty') and is a poetic adjective/noun appended at the end.
segments.s388-g19: چنین پاسخش داد اسفندیار = 'Thus he gave his answer, Esfandiyar.' چنین ('thus') fronts the clause; پاسخش is پاسخ + enclitic -ش 'his/his answer'; داد is the past verb 'gave'; اسفندیار is the agent placed after the verb (a permissible classical/Persian word order: object/verb/subject or adverb-object-verb-subject).
segments.s388-g20: که خشنود بادا ز من شهریار = reported content introduced by که. خشنود is the adjective 'pleased,' بادا is an archaic optative/third-person formula meaning 'may it be' (expressing wish), ز من here means 'because of / with respect to me' (poetic 'from me' = 'with me' or 'in regard to me'), and شهریار is the noun 'the king' (the one hoped to be pleased). The whole clause expresses a wish: 'may the king be pleased with me.'
segments.s388-g21: مرا آن بود تخت و تاج و سپاه = an idiomatic/archaic copular construction. مرا (here 'to me' or an ethical/object-marking form) + آن بود ('that was'), followed by a list of desired things: تخت و تاج و سپاه ('throne and crown and army'). In older Persian this order conveys 'what I wanted/for me it was ...' with آن serving as a demonstrative placeholder subject and بود as the copula 'was.'
segments.s388-g22: که خشنود باشد جهاندار شاه = subordinate clause introduced by که expressing purpose/result. خشنود 'pleased' + باشد is the present-subjunctive/optative form 'may he be / let him be' (expressing desire), جهاندار شاه 'the sovereign king' is the hoped-for beneficiary/subject. Together: 'that the sovereign king may be pleased.'
segments.s388-g23: جهاندار داند که بر دشت رزم = 'The sovereign knows that on the plain/field of battle...' جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign,' داند is the present tense 'knows' (classical form), که introduces the subordinate content clause, and بر دشت رزم is a locative phrase ('on/over the battlefield/plain of combat') with بر as the poetic preposition 'on/upon.'
segments.s388-g24: چو من دیدم افگنده روی گرزم = 'when he saw me, he [had] cast down his face at my mace.' چو = 'when'; here دیدم should be read in classical/poetic syntax as 'he saw me' (the object pronoun is encoded in the verbal form or contextually supplied), افگنده is a participle 'having cast/downcast' modifying the (understood) subject, روی = 'his face,' and گرزم is 'my mace' (گرز 'mace' with possessive enclitic). The clause therefore describes the sovereign's reaction on seeing the speaker.
segments.s388-g113: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; کوه is its object 'mountain'. اندر آمد is the simple past 'came in/entered' (verb phrase) and, in this poetic inversion, the verb precedes the subject. سپاه بزرگ is the noun phrase 'a great host/army' (subject) placed after the verb.
segments.s388-g114: This is an epithet and name in apposition to 'سپاه بزرگ': جهانگیر is the epithet 'world‑conqueror' before the name, اسفندیار is the proper name, and سترگ is a descriptive noun/adjective 'of mighty sight/gaze' placed after the name (poetic postpositive epithet).
segments.s388-g115: چو here introduces a temporal clause 'when'. لشکر is the object 'army' and بیاراست is the past verb 'arrayed/arrayed up'; the subject اسفندیار follows the verb in inverted, poetic order (i.e. 'when the army he arrayed — Esfandiyar').
segments.s388-g116: جهان شد is 'the world became' (شد = become). به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner of/like' (به functioning as the marker '‑ly/in the way of'). دریای is the genitive 'sea of …' and قار is used metaphorically as a harsh cry/clamour — together 'like a sea of clamour/roar'.
segments.s388-g117: بشد is a past/resultive 'it became/it happened' (here 'there came/there gathered'), گرد 'around' indicates surrounding, and بستور 'thus/in this manner' is an adverbial modifier. پور زریر is the patronymic/name 'Pour‑Zarir' (son of Zarir); the whole clause means 'thus around him came Pour‑Zarir.'
segments.s388-g118: که introduces a relative clause modifying the preceding name. بگذاشتی is 2nd‑person past 'you had left/you left'; بیشه 'thicket/wood' is its locative. ز + و here represent an archaic contraction meaning 'از او' (from/of him / 'him'), and نره شیر is an appositive 'a male lion' describing the person left there (i.e. 'whom you had left in the thicket — a male lion').
segments.s388-g119: بیاراست is the past 'he arrayed/arranged'. بر میمنه 'on the right (flank)' is a locative prepositional phrase, and جای خویش 'his own place/position' uses خوی + the possessive suffix ش to form the possessive 'his own'. The verb governs what follows (what he placed on that side).
segments.s388-g120: This coordinated noun phrase is the object of بیاراست: سپهبد is 'spahbed/commander', بد is an epithet literally 'bad/ill' (here functioning as a descriptive epithet attached to the commander), و is 'and' linking the two coordinated items, لشکر آرای خویش is 'his army‑array' (لشکر 'army' + آرای 'array/ornament' forming the idea of well‑arrayed troops) with خویش (خوی + ش) marking possession 'his own'.
segments.s388-g185: گرفته is a past/perfect participle meaning “having taken” or simply “took/ seized” and here functions as the main verbal action placed before its object (poetic inversion). کمان کیانی is a noun followed by a postpositive epithet/possessor (کیانی = “Kayanian/royal”), a normal Persian pattern where the modifier follows the noun. به چنگ is a prepositional phrase of manner (به + چنگ) meaning “into the grip / in his hand”; in this idiom چنگ (literally “claw”) is used metaphorically for “grip.”
segments.s388-g186: This is an asyndetic list (no verb repeated): یکی is the numeral/indefinite “one/a single,” followed by a string of weapon nouns where پولاد (steel) functions as a material adjective modifying تیر/پیکان; پیکان here duplicates/clarifies the notion of a missile/arrow. خدنگ is another weapon (a short sword/dagger) added at the end of the list. The verb from the previous segment supplies the action for these objects (he seized/took them).
segments.s388-g187: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” نزدیک شد is a past intransitive clause “he became/grew near” (شد here = 'became'). راند is a past tense verb “drove/put (in)” whose direct object is left implicit (the arrow); اندر کمان is a prepositional phrase “into/on the bow.” The clause sequence is slightly inverted for poetic effect, with the temporal clause first and the action after it.
segments.s388-g188: بزد is past “he struck/smote.” The repeated preposition بر (بر بر) is a poetic construction: the first بر can govern an implied object (omitted because context supplies it) and the second بر attaches to the explicit following noun; و here is the simple coordinating conjunction “and/then.” سینه پهلوان is a noun plus postposed possessor/title (“the champion’s breast”), the normal Persian order for possession in older/poetic style.
segments.s388-g189: ز is the poetic preposition “from.” زین is the noun “saddle.” اندر is another locative preposition (“in/onto/within”) used here with the motion verb. آویخت is the past intransitive “he hung (down)/clung” and the proper name اسفندیار follows the verb as the subject by poetic inversion (verb before subject). The whole sequence means “Esfandiar hung down from the saddle.”
segments.s388-g190: ب + دان here combine as a poetic/archaic compound equivalent to 'بدان' (’with that / thereby’); تا introduces a purpose/result clause (“so that / in order that”). گمانی is an abstract noun “a conjecture / suspicion.” برد is used here in the sense “took/received” (i.e. ‘took a notion’) and گرگسار is the person affected (Gorgesar) but appears after the verb in the poetic word order. The clause as a whole says that by that action Gorgesar came to form a (mis)conception.
segments.s388-g191: که is the subordinating conjunction “that.” آن تیر is “that arrow.” بگذشت is the past of گذشت meaning “passed by / went past” (here describing the arrow’s flight). بر + جوشنش is the prepositional phrase “on/against his cuirass,” with جوشن (armor) bearing the possessive enclitic -ش “his.” Word order places the verb before the object as a common poetic option.
segments.s388-g192: بخست is the past of بستن in the sense “closed / fastened / (here) smote/shut.” آن کیانی is the nominal phrase “the Kayanian/royal one” (آن = demonstrative + کیانی = epithet), placed after the verb as subject by inversion. بر روشنش is a prepositional phrase “upon his روشن (lit. ‘bright/shining’),” where روشنش is the noun روشن with the possessive suffix -ش; in epic diction روشن can refer to a shining part (helmet, surface, or breastplate).
segments.s388-g145: نیاید is the negative present form ('does not come/appear') and stands before the noun phrase as is common in poetic Persian. پدیدار پیروزئی is a noun phrase 'the appearance (پدیدار) of victory (پیروزئی)'; the verb نیاید negates that whole phrase: 'the appearance of victory does not come.'
segments.s388-g146: نکو is an adjective 'good/fair' modifying an implied person or quality; رفتنی‌گر combines رفتنی ('about to go/going') with the conditional/optative suffix -گر, producing 'if he is about to go' or 'being about to depart'. دل افروزئی ('heart‑delighting' / 'that which brightens the heart') is an attributive noun phrase describing the beloved/hero; overall the clause reads 'if the good one is about to leave (رفتنی‌گر), [then] the heart‑delighting [presence]...' i.e. conditional link to the preceding negative verb.
segments.s388-g147: خود و ویژگان is a coordinated noun phrase 'he himself and the distinguished/choicemen' (ویژگان postpositive 'the distinguished'). بر هیونان is a prepositional phrase where بر means 'against' here, and مست is an adjectival predicate 'intoxicated/frenzied' used figuratively: 'furious against the beasts.' Word order places the predicate after the prepositional phrase, typical of poetic style.
segments.s388-g148: بسازیم is a verbal form 'we/they make/prepare' (here narrative: 'made ready' or 'prepared'); باهستگی is an adverbial noun 'with being/presence' (i.e. 'with their company/presence'); راه جست uses جست (literally 'leapt/sprung') with راه as its object to mean 'took to the road' or 'set out.' The verb and its modifiers follow the poetic ordering.
segments.s388-g149: چو here is a temporal conjunction 'when.' اسفندیار is the proper name. از میان دو صف is a prepositional phrase 'from out of/between the two lines' (میان = 'between', صف = 'battle line'), so the whole clause sets the time/location: 'When Esfandiyar came out from between the two ranks.'
segments.s388-g150: The initial چو here is a simile marker 'like/as.' پیل ژیان is 'a living elephant' (پیل 'elephant' + ژیان 'life' used postpositively to mean 'living'). بر لب آورده کف is a compact phrase: بر لب 'on (his) lip', آورده 'has brought/borne' (perfect participle), and کف 'foam' — idiomatically 'had foam at his lips.' The participle follows the phrase it governs, as typical in classical Persian.
segments.s388-g151: همی is an iterative adverb 'kept on/continued.' گشت (from گشتن) here functions as a narrative auxiliary 'turned/kept on' and combines with برسان ('to bring/bring forth') to mean 'kept on bringing' or 'continued to summon.' گردان سپهر is a noun phrase 'the troop/host of the heavens' (گردان 'troop', سپهر 'heavens') and is the object of برسان.
segments.s388-g152: به چنگ اندرون is an instrumental/locative phrase 'into/with the inner hand/claw' (چنگ 'claw/hand', اندرون 'inner'). گرزه گاو چهر is a noun phrase where گرزه 'mace' is qualified by گاو چهر 'bull‑faced' (گاو 'bull/cow', چهر 'face'), i.e. 'the mace with a bull's face.' Together they describe the instrument he seized: 'with his inner hand he seized the bull‑faced mace.'
segments.s388-g257: ز خون = a poetic prepositional phrase 'with/from blood' placed before the noun phrase. در کفش خنجر = a prepositional phrase 'the dagger in (his) shoe' where در + noun precedes the head noun (poetic/archaic ordering). افسرده بود = adjective + past copula 'was'; افسرده is a predicative adjective describing the dagger (here used metaphorically).
segments.s388-g258: بر و کتفش = two coordinated terms ('بر' and 'کتفش') forming a compound of body-parts (connected by و); کتفش is 'his shoulder' with the possessive suffix -ش. از جوش = 'because of/from a boil/pustule' where از marks the cause. آزرده بود = adjectival predicate 'was afflicted/distressed' with بود as the past copula.
segments.s388-g259: بشستند is the past plural 'they washed'. شمشیر و کفش are the direct objects coordinated by و ('sword and shoe'). به شیر indicates the medium or liquid used ('in/with milk'); in classical/poetic Persian the prepositional phrase can follow the objects.
segments.s388-g260: کشیدند is past plural 'they drew/pulled'. بیرون is an adverb 'out'. ز marks origin 'from', followed by خفتانش ('his quiver/sheath' — here a noun with possessive -ش). تیر is the direct object 'arrow' at the end. Word order is verb … adverb … prepositional phrase … object, typical in poetry.
segments.s388-g261: به آب اندر آمد = 'came into the water' where به + noun (toward/into) plus the archaism اندر ('into/within') precede آمد 'came'. سر و تن بشست = 'he washed head and body' with بشست 'he washed' and the coordinated objects سر و تن following the verb (poetic order).
segments.s388-g262: جهانجوی is a compound noun 'world-seeker' (an epithet). شادان modifies دل (joyful of heart) and درست qualifies تن ('sound/whole body'); the phrase therefore presents two coordinated results/qualities: joyful heart and sound body. Adjectives and qualities in classical Persian can follow the nouns they describe or form compact attributive phrases as here.
segments.s388-g263: یکی = 'a certain' (indefinite subject). جامه سوکواران = 'the garment of the archers' (possession/association; classical Persian often places the possessor after the possessed). بخواست is the past 'he asked/desired' (verb at the end of the clause in poetic word order).
segments.s388-g264: بیامد = 'he came' (past). بر داور = 'to the judge' where بر functions as a prepositional marker 'toward/to' and داور is 'judge'. داد و راست = two nouns 'justice and right/fairness' (objects/aims of his coming); the clause is elliptical — 'he came to the judge for justice and right.'
segments.s388-g233: به ایرانیان گفت is a prepositional indirect-object + verb: به + ایرانیان = 'to the Iranians', followed by گفت (past tense 'he said'). شمشیر جنگ is a noun phrase 'war-sword' (شمشیر 'sword' + جنگ 'war/battle') that here functions as the object/topic of the command that follows; the object is placed before the verb مدارید (in classical word order the commanded action comes after its object).
segments.s388-g234: مدارید is a 2nd-person-plural imperative/optative form used as a command ('hold back / refrain / restrain' in context); خیره گرفته is a compound participial phrase (خیره + گرفته) meaning 'having been gripped/fixed' or 'grasping tightly' (گرفته = past participle 'taken, seized'); به چنگ is a prepositional phrase 'in (the) hand/grip' (چنگ here = grip/handful). Together they give the command image: 'hold back (your) war-swords, having gripped them firmly in hand.'
segments.s388-g235: نیام is the noun 'scabbard/sheath' placed as the affected object; از + دل و خون دشمن is a source/comitative phrase 'from the heart and blood of the enemy' (از marks origin/material). کنید is the 2nd-person-plural imperative 'do/make' — here commanding that the scabbards be made/filled/soaked with enemy heart and blood (literary war-image). Note the object (نیام) appears before the verb (کنید) in classical style.
segments.s388-g236: ز (poetic form of از) marks source/means 'from/out of'; تورانیان is the source group 'the Turanians'; کوه قارن is a noun phrase 'mountain Qaran' (a simile/standard of height or heap). کنید again is the 2pl imperative 'make' — the construction 'ز X کوه Y کنید' means 'from X make a mountain like Y', i.e. heap up the Turanians until they form a mountain like Qaran.
segments.s388-g237: بیفشارد is a past-tense verb 'pressed/squeezed/pressed on' (classical form). ران لشکر is a genitival/nominal phrase where ران ('thigh/rear/flank') qualifies لشکر ('army') — 'the flanks/rear of the host' or more generally 'the host's ranks'; کینه‌خواه is a postpositive adjective/noun 'vengeful/avenging' that characterizes the host. Word order places the verb before its affected phrase in a compressed, poetic way.
segments.s388-g238: سپاه is subject 'the army'; اندر آمد is a two-part verbal locution (اندر 'in/among' + آمد 'came') meaning 'came in/advanced into'; به پیش سپاه is a locative phrase 'before / in front of the (other) army' — together meaning 'the army came up in front of (met) the opposing army.' The repetition of سپاه indicates the two opposing hosts.
segments.s388-g239: به خون غرقه شد is a passive/resultative clause: به خون = 'in/with blood', غرقه = 'drenched/immersed', شد = past auxiliary 'became/was' — together 'was drenched in blood'. خاک و سنگ و گیا is a series of nouns ('earth, stone, and plant/grass') serving as the grammatical subject that follows the predicate in this poetic order.
segments.s388-g240: بگشتس is an archaic past form 'it turned/it was turned' (3rd-person singular). بخون‌گر is a compound agentive/adjectival 'blood-maker' or 'blood-stained' describing what results; بدی آسیا is a genitival/attributive phrase 'the mill of evil/destruction' (بدی qualifying آسیا 'mill') — together the clause pictures the mill of destruction turning and becoming gory: 'the mill of evil turned, a bringer of blood.' The sequence is poetically compressed: verb + descriptive compounds forming a single image.
